author: @MartinBacon-o9b
text: HI couple of questions 1) how did you get the wheel head off ? 2) did you have to change the leather wheel and how would you do that ?

author: @richardlidstone-scott2452
text: Hi, great video. Any idea on make and model of the wheel? I have one and need some spares.

author: @TonyFlisch
text: I have an identical wheel which runs OK, but at certain speeds it clunks - I suspect and irregularity on the flywheel - it looks like the drive wheel that slides along the shaft  on yours is rubber coated whereas mine is leather  yes leather!   Sadly the video shows the cosmetic stuff and the bit I really wanted to see was the drive mechanism.  I guess I'll have to experiment myself and I was so excited to see that the video exists.  Ho hum - back to Google search!

author: @Julkkis1980
text:
With epoxy resin, standard wheat flour works really well.. Built a few boats using it.  Also,   i dont use the fancy stuff. Epoxy for concrete floors does mighty fine   just as a tip.

Thank you for all of the info i have gotten from you and no mean to sound disrespectful.

author: @duanesharon
text: Rob I want to sincerely say Thank You for this vid. It gave in my case my marriage a second chance. I appreciate all your vids great work ✔️

author: @William_Hada
text: Great refurb job! Looks fantastic! Looks like a well-built piece of equipment and made to last. Definitely worth the effort!

author: @peteroneill2991
text: Nice referb. and philosophy Rob.

author: @Buzzhumma
text: I won’t give up on you buddy 😂

author: @stuffoflardohfortheloveof
text: Bloody hell Rob this is timely. My partner bought me a birthday gift of a days ‘introduction to pottery’ a while back but because of COVID it was rescheduled twice but I ended up going yesterday......had a great day, no clay aspirations I’m afraid and it is much, much harder than it looks (try simply centring the damn clay). Being me I asked loads of questions including having a look at the construction of her many wheels (just like yours) and was amazed at how much they cost (potentially thousands).......I’ve been thinking about this all night and then get up to see you refurbing one. I know of at least one person who would buy it off you! (Not me - I proved that yesterday 😂). 👍

author: @whig01
text: A new potter's wheel is under $200, though. How much labor has to be put into this?

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: But there's so much more value to restoring this than simply buying something new, and it's so much more meaningful when you're done.  😊

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no it isn't mate - a student quality wheel that can handle a kilo or so of clay will cost you $200 - a professional wheel that can handle much larger amounts will set you back $1,500 to $2,000  - this is a professional quality wheel - but that is really beside the point

replies.author: @whig01
replies.text: I'd guess that would come down to motor power. If you've got a full horsepower or more there, then good on you.

author: @ryanjames1501
text: Very nice repair work! On wood I always use Cold Glue (wood glue) and sawdust mixed in a ratio that it is similar to soft dough. Fills all cracks and you can even slowly build up / mould something that you can't make with wood.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I do too - but this is going to be in a wet environment - clay work uses a lot of water - so I figured something a little more waterproof

replies.author: @ryanjames1501
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  I use a bit of Woodoc Marine sealer on all my projects when I am done, but your reasoning is good as you then don't need the extra step.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @ryanjames1501 nice one mate - to be honest either is good and your way would allow swelling and shrinking

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Wondering if that wood is Teak. That is what springs to mind if I needed something that was going to repeatedly go from dry to wet and back again. It is very expensive these days. They decked ships with it and lab benches.

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: Teak is one of the woods they used to use as ballast in the old sailing ships!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah I wondered that too - it's a bit red for teak though

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering There is a whole world of woods and what each is good (and bad) for. They used to make wooden ploughs with a Pear wood separator. Oak is great for construction (look at the beams in a church) but with age they produce an acid, turning the wood black (and corroding any metals on the surface)!

author: @angelusmendez5084
text: Awesome work! With that flywheel and the motor it's useful even as a generator.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It sure is!

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Rob’s part of the world is beginning to seem like the gadget equivalent of where elephants go to die. I hardly ever see such things left abandoned and unwanted in my area. Think I might go on safari over Rob’s way next time I feel like doing a bit of salvage hunting.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: more than welcome mate - you bring the biscuits and I will supply the tea lol
